title: Catechist Guide — Community: Living the Faith Together
html:
Catechist Guide

To prepare and lead the class. For the exclusive use of the catechist.

Class Summary

Estimated duration: 60 minutes

Central theme: The human person, created for communion, is called to live the faith in community: from the family to civil society, through the Church, according to the principles of the common good, subsidiarity, and solidarity.

Objectives:

  • Understand that social life is a requirement of human nature, not an addition (CCC 1879).
  • Recognize the family as the original cell of social life and the foundation of the fourth commandment (CCC 2207).
  • Know the principles of Catholic social teaching: the common good, subsidiarity, and solidarity (CCC 1906-1943).
  • Identify the duties of citizens and the right to conscientious objection (CCC 2239-2243).
  • Discover that faith is necessarily lived in community: the Church as the Body of Christ.

Key verse: “All the believers were together and had everything in common” (Acts 2:44)

Catechism references: CCC 2207-2257, 2443-2463

Suggested Class Structure

Section Duration What the Catechist Does
1. The Big Question 8 min Read the account of the Jerusalem community (Acts 2:42-47). Ask: “Why can’t I be a Christian alone?”
2. The family: social cell 12 min Explain the fourth commandment. Mutual duties between parents and children. Show the hidden life of Nazareth as a model.
3. Common good, subsidiarity, solidarity 12 min The three pillars of Catholic social doctrine. Explain each one with concrete, relatable examples.
4. Political community and the Church 8 min Duties of the citizen. Conscientious objection. The Church-State relationship as service to the human person.
5. For Reflection 8 min Share in pairs or small groups the reflection questions. Brief group sharing.
6. Closing: Key Idea + Prayer 5 min Read the Key Idea. Connection to real life. Pray together the closing prayer.
7. Activity for the week 7 min Hand out the workbook. Explain the activities (community map, letter to parents, opening a door).

Notes for the Catechist

The Early Christian Community

The portrait of Acts 2:42-47 is the ideal of Christian community. It is not an unattainable utopia; it is what the Holy Spirit made possible at Pentecost. Stress the four pillars: the apostles’ teaching (doctrine), fellowship (koinonia), the breaking of bread (Eucharist), and prayers. These four elements remain the backbone of every authentic Christian community. The community of Jerusalem “enjoyed the favor of all the people” because their love was visible, concrete, attractive. That is the vocation of every parish, every faith group.

The Family: Don’t Idealize It

When speaking of the family as the “original cell of social life,” avoid two extremes: idealizing it (as though all families were perfect) and despising it (as though it were an obsolete institution). Many participants may come from broken, conflictive, or painful families. The Church’s teaching speaks of what the family is called to be, not what each concrete family achieves. Nazareth was an imperfect community (Joseph thought of divorcing Mary, they lost Jesus in the Temple) sustained by faith. That is what our families are like: imperfect but called to holiness. The message is not: “your family must be perfect.” It is: “your family can be a place of encounter with God, despite its wounds.”

Common Good, Subsidiarity, Solidarity: The Three Pillars

These three principles are inseparable. The common good without subsidiarity becomes oppressive statism (the State decides what is good for everyone). Subsidiarity without solidarity becomes selfish individualism (everyone for themselves). Solidarity without the common good becomes ineffectual sentimentalism. They must be kept together. Use concrete examples close to the participants’ reality.

Conscientious Objection: A Sensitive Topic

The right to disobey unjust laws is fundamental to Catholic morality, but it must be explained with balance. Not every law I dislike is unjust; only a law that contradicts objective moral law is unjust. The martyrs did not disobey out of whim or political ideology; they disobeyed because the alternative was to sin. If questions arise about controversial topics (abortion, euthanasia, same-sex “marriage”), respond with doctrinal clarity but pastoral charity. Do not condemn persons; explain why certain laws contradict human dignity and why the Christian must bear witness to the truth even at the cost of social rejection.

The Church and Politics: Neither Confusion Nor Indifference

Explain carefully the Church-State relationship: the Church is not a political party nor should it be, but it has the right and duty to illuminate the conscience of the faithful and all people regarding the moral demands of social life. Christ’s distinction (“give to Caesar what is Caesar’s and to God what is God’s”) is not hostile separation but ordered autonomy: temporal realities have their own laws, but they are not independent of God. The lay Catholic has the specific vocation of sanctifying temporal structures from within.

Cultural Sensitivity

Take advantage of culturally relevant examples. The Mexican tequio as subsidiarity and solidarity in action, the posadas as a school of hospitality and community, Our Lady of Guadalupe as the builder of a people. These examples are not folkloric adornments: they are concrete incarnations of the universal principles of Catholic social teaching. If your participants are not Mexican, find equivalent examples from their own culture.

Adaptation by Age

🌱 Seed (children 7-12)

Tell the story of the first Christians as a tale: “Imagine that all your friends and their families decide to live together, share everything, and help each other every day.” Read Acts 2:42-47 with expression. Activity: draw the Jerusalem community (people praying, sharing bread, helping one another). Ask: “Who makes up your community?” (family, school, catechesis). Explain the fourth commandment with simple examples: “How do you honor dad and mom? Helping at home, obeying, thanking them.” Song: “We are a pilgrim people” or equivalent.

🌿 Shoots (teens 13-17)

Connect with their experience: loneliness online, the need for belonging, peer pressure. Opening question: “How many followers do you have on social media, and how many would you call at 3 a.m. if you had a serious problem?” Explore the difference between followers and real community. Touch on conscientious objection: “Have you faced situations where you were asked to do something you knew was wrong? What did you do?” Use the example of The Mission: what would you do to defend your community? Dialogue about what they can do as young people to build community in their parish or school.

🪴 Root (adults new to the faith)

Many adults come from religious individualism: “I believe in God my own way, I don’t need the Church.” This class is an answer to that attitude. Recall that the Church is not an optional club but the Body of Christ. Use the Pauline image: a member separated from the body dies (1 Cor 12). Speak of subsidiarity applied to daily life: the family, the business, the municipality. Invite sharing of community experiences that have been transformative. Ask: “What has the faith community given you that you wouldn’t have achieved alone?”

🌳 Tree (formed adults)

Deepen into Catholic social teaching as an organic body of thought. Walk through history: Rerum Novarum (1891), Quadragesimo Anno (1931), Mater et Magistra (1961), Pacem in Terris (1963), Populorum Progressio (1967), Laborem Exercens (1981), Sollicitudo Rei Socialis (1987), Centesimus Annus (1991), Evangelium Vitae (1995), Caritas in Veritate (2009), Laudato Si’ (2015), Fratelli Tutti (2020). Discuss the relationship between Catholic social thought and modern ideologies: economic liberalism, socialism, environmentalism. Catholic social doctrine is not a third way between capitalism and socialism, but a radically different proposal founded on the dignity of the person. Debate the concept of the “consistent ethic of life”: is it really possible to maintain it without contradictions?

Supplementary Resources

  • YouCat: Questions 320-330 (“What does the Church teach about social life?”)
  • Catechism of the Catholic Church: 1877-1948 (the person and society), 2196-2257 (fourth commandment), 2443-2463 (love for the poor).
  • Compendium of the Social Doctrine of the Church: Pontifical Council for Justice and Peace, 2004. Essential reading for those who wish to go deeper.
  • Film: “The Mission” (Roland Joffé, 1986). The Jesuit reductions as Christian community confronting colonial power.
  • Film: “Romero” (John Duigan, 1989). The life of Archbishop Óscar Romero and his defense of the Salvadoran community.
  • Documentary: “Karol: A Pope Who Changed History,” on Saint John Paul II and Solidarity’s role in the fall of communism.
  • Book: G.K. Chesterton, “What’s Wrong with the World” and “The Everlasting Man,” for the distributist vision.
  • Art: Paintings of the Jesuit reductions at the Museum of Hispano-American Art in Buenos Aires.
  • Witness: Archbishop Óscar Romero, homilies and pastoral letters.

Difficult Questions

“Isn’t it enough to believe in God in my own way? Why do I need the Church?”

Because faith is not a private experience. Christ founded a Church, not a collection of isolated believers. The sacraments, the proclaimed Word, the communion of saints: all this is received in the Church. “No one can have God as Father who does not have the Church as Mother” (Saint Cyprian). God saves us as a people, not as isolated individuals. You can pray in your room, but you need to receive Christ in the Eucharist, and that happens in the community gathered by the priest. The Church is not a hindrance to your relationship with God: it is the means willed by God for your salvation.

“If authority comes from God, what about corrupt or dictatorial governments?”

That authority in the abstract comes from God does not mean that every concrete ruler is willed by God or that their acts are legitimate. Authority is only legitimate when it seeks the common good through morally licit means (CCC 1903). A government that oppresses, steals, or kills has lost its moral legitimacy. Christians are not obliged to obey unjust laws (CCC 2242). And in extreme cases, the traditional doctrine recognizes the right to active resistance against tyranny, provided very strict conditions are met (grave and prolonged harm, impossibility of other recourse, reasonable probability of success, no greater evils provoked).

“Isn’t Catholic social teaching just a disguised left-wing ideology?”

No. Catholic social doctrine is neither left nor right. It defends legitimate private property (against socialism) but subordinates it to the universal destination of goods (against savage capitalism). It defends market freedom but demands that it be at the service of the person. It defends the unborn but also the immigrant and the poor. If it sometimes “sounds left-wing,” it is because the world has normalized injustices that the Gospel denounces. And if it sometimes “sounds right-wing,” it is because the Gospel defends natural realities—family, property, authority—that certain ideologies want to destroy. The Church weds itself to no political system; it judges all from Christ.

“What if my parents are abusive or have hurt me deeply? Must I still honor them?”

The fourth commandment requires honoring parents, but “honor” does not mean submitting to abuse or justifying evil. You can honor a parent who has wounded you by recognizing that they gave you life (an objective good, however much they may have tarnished it), by praying for their conversion, by forgiving them in your heart (forgiveness is not approving evil or renouncing justice). But you have the right to protect yourself, to place distance if necessary, and to seek help. The Church never asks anyone to remain in an abusive situation out of a false sense of filial duty. God wants your good, not your destruction.

Catechist’s Prayer

Lord Jesus Christ,
who at the Last Supper asked the Father
that we all be one as you and the Father are one,
grant me to be an instrument of communion.
May I, in teaching about community,
transmit not only doctrine but witness.
Give me wisdom to explain
the principles of your social teaching,
and love to bring them close to the concrete lives
of those who listen to me.
May this class not remain mere words,
but build true community
among us.
Amen.

Print Catechist Guide
